3. David Ondrej's playbook is Fable as the boss, cheap models as the hands. The most concrete "how to actually use this without going broke" came from David Ondrej, "Fable 5 is back... here is my plan", who runs Fable as the orchestrator and hands the grunt work to cheaper models.

  • Fable plans, the cheap models execute. It does architecture, competitor analysis, and PR reviews as the planner, while Kimi K2.7, GLM 5.2, MiniMax, and DeepSeek V4 do the actual coding at 7x to 25x less cost. Do not burn Fable limits fixing a front-end button.
  • Use it through the API, not the chat. He says the API is less restricted and only got silently downgraded to Opus a handful of times across hundreds of prompts.
  • Delete most of your skills. He argues Fable needs so little hand-holding that 80% to 100% of your old skill files are now dead weight.

One caution. Ondrej also promotes prompt tricks to dodge the safety classifiers and open distillation (training a cheaper model on a stronger one's outputs) that violates the terms of service, and he notes over 100 Fable-distillation datasets already sit on Hugging Face. Take the workflow, skip the parts that break the rules.

4. The number behind all the gating: someone just called Anthropic a $3 trillion company. On All-In, "Anthropic is worth HOW MUCH!?", one of the hosts argued Anthropic would trade around $3 trillion as a public company today, on revenue "well over $100 billion" this year and 85% gross margins on inference (the cost of actually running the model to answer, as opposed to training it). His frame is that the market has already absorbed the number and it is simply shifting from private to public. Take the figure with heavy salt, but it explains the behavior around it. MGX out of Abu Dhabi just closed a $49B AI fund, and Microsoft stood up a 6,000-person "Frontier" unit with $2.5B. When people believe those margins, they build like this.

5. Owning your own intelligence is turning into a real capex line. The through-line from Ondrej and the open-weight crowd is that self-hosting is becoming a genuine spend, not a hobby. He is personally putting $50,000 to $100,000 into a local GPU cluster and a 40TB-to-80TB NAS to archive open-weight models and datasets before they can be pulled, and he is telling people to buy more VRAM and Nvidia's Spark. Nous Research shipped Hermes v0.18 with a mixture-of-agents design (several models drafting and judging each other's answers) in the same spirit. The bet is that per-person token consumption keeps going exponential, and the people spending real money on it want their intelligence on hardware they own.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Anthropic's own prompt-engineering roundtable with Amanda Askell and the team is a genuinely good primer (the temp-agency-worker metaphor for prompts, giving the model an explicit out for edge cases), but it is evergreen technique with no this-week action, so I am holding it. The "automate trend research across nine platforms" workflow is a content-marketing build, thin for an investor. The Anthropic $3 trillion valuation and the self-hosting capex trend cross to Investing. The export-control policy angle and the Anthropic-Samsung chip also live in News as a compute-geopolitics story.

item.title: [Investing] Micron got flushed, and for once every desk is buying the same dip
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory pullback is being bought hard, Bitcoin is bleeding under $60K, and Strategy just traded below its own stack for the first time ever.
item.content:encoded:
The memory pullback is being bought hard, Bitcoin is bleeding under $60K, and Strategy just traded below its own stack for the first time ever.

Yesterday was the flush. This morning is the argument about what it meant, and the loudest voices have already answered it: they bought.

Into the open. Cash markets are open today and closed tomorrow, Friday July 3, for Independence Day, so today is a pre-holiday session with thin afternoon liquidity. Two real catalysts land in that shortened window. The June jobs report was pulled forward to today ahead of the holiday, so the number hits a market already jumpy about whether the Fed hikes at all this year. And Tesla ($TSLA) reports Q2 deliveries, where the estimate gap is enormous: the Street sits near 406k against Troy Teslike at 466k, and $425 is the level that matters. Watch whether $MU reclaims its 21-day moving average (the short-term trend line it just lost) and whether the $SOX (the Philadelphia semis index) steadies after yesterday's 6%-plus gash. Watch $BTC and the $60K line. Oil stayed soft with WTI near $68. Size for a holiday tape, not a trend day.

1. The memory dip is the most crowded buy on the board. This is the one to lead with, and not because I am sure it is right. It is because I have rarely seen this many independent desks pile onto one trade at the same moment. Crowding like that either front-runs a real move or marks the top, and here I lean front-run. Here is why.

After the semis got taken to the woodshed yesterday, the Stocks list lit up. Marcos Milla called $MU "under $1,000 equals smash buy" while warning leverage could still shake out 30% in three days. Milk Road Macro is adding with a "$MU to $4,000" target. Melvin booked a 144% gain and is staying long. $MU closed below its 21-day moving average for the first time since April, which the chartists read as a reset, not a breakdown.

The fundamental spine is the strongest part of the argument. In the inbox, Markos laid out the HBM (high-bandwidth memory, the fast memory stacked right next to the GPU) math that matters. Efficiency gains are running around 5% a year and decelerating, so you would need a 25% jump on top of the 30% already priced to flip 2027-28 into oversupply. Cheaper memory tiers add capacity but cannot replace HBM, because the data a model is actively using has to sit next to the chip for speed. His keeper line: no GPU generation has ever shipped with less memory per system, 80GB to 288GB and climbing. That is Jevons paradox in silicon.

CEO Sanjay Mehrotra is selling the same durability story with receipts: 16 take-or-pay agreements (the customer pays whether or not it takes the chips) running 2026 to 2030, 14 of them carrying a $100B cumulative revenue minimum against $22B in customer cash deposits. Even the Reddit desk reads the pullback as healthy consolidation after names ran two to three times, not a cycle peak.

Here is the tell that makes me take it seriously rather than fade it. Kobeissi flagged that retail is now just 6% of Mag 7 volume, a four-year low and down 79% from the April 2025 peak, with the money rotating out of the megacaps and straight into semiconductors. That is not a froth signal, that is a rotation. My read: the dip is buyable, but the crowding is real, so scale in and keep the 21-day reclaim as your confirmation rather than chasing the first green candle.

2. Marvell is the "next bottleneck" everyone is pre-positioning into. Milk Road's follow-on to the memory trade is $MRVL, and the thesis is clean: the bottleneck arc runs compute to memory to connectivity, and Marvell owns the connectivity leg. The CXL memory-fabric market goes from $1.3B to $7-10B by 2030, Marvell bought XConn for $540M to lead it, and management guides CXL revenue to $1B in FY27 and $2B in FY28. Custom silicon is the bigger number: 18 programs, 12 of them at top-four hyperscalers, an FY27 guide of $11.5B up 40%, and a path to $10B-plus by FY29. The risk is not the story, it is the price and the concentration, Amazon is 30-40% of revenue and $AVGO is coming for the same sockets. This is a position-builder's name, not a chase.

3. Bitcoin is risk capital squared, and the AI vacuum is the reason. $BTC is grinding lower, closed below its monthly 50 and weekly 200, and keeps refusing to hold $60K. The datapoint that stops you cold is on the Crypto list: Strategy ($MSTR) just traded below the value of its own Bitcoin for the first time ever. Michael Saylor holds more than 800,000 BTC and the stock is down about 80%, and he is out talking his book with a coherent story, that the "AI black hole" pulled $10-20B out of crypto and $500B into the AI IPO complex, that Bitcoin's dominance (its share of the total crypto market cap) is back near 70% from 41% in 2021, and that Bitcoin is now undervalued. The bears do not disagree on the mechanism, they just are not waiting around: Novogratz says crypto "lost its sponsorship," and David Hoffman sold his $ETH as Ethereum's smart-contract dominance fell from over 90% to 60%. Levels: $60K is the line, a final flush to $50-55K is on the table, and you do not get a confirmed bottom until a higher high above $67K and a reclaim of the 200-day. Stocks with Josh is buying that fear with a weekly death cross (the 50-week average slipping under the 200-week, classically bearish) as his signal, a $56K target zone and a deeper add near $44K. The one alt with a real bid is $SOL, where Ignas (the SIMD-547 base-fee burn turning it deflationary), andyyy, and Nebraskangooner independently called a bottom. If Bitcoin pukes again, Solana goes with it. Own it for the upside, not the protection.

4. The macro regime quietly changed, and almost nobody is pricing it. The sharpest macro take of the morning came from Eric Wallerstein: the Warsh Fed kills "kangaroo macro," the print-to-print obsession where every data release is a trade. Warsh pledged 2% inflation eight times in one press conference and bond yields fell, and the new game is cycles and trends over reacting to a single jobs number, especially with post-Covid data reliability degraded. That reframes today's jobs report as noise inside a trend, not a verdict. The risk-off tells are still worth respecting: Kalshi prices a 50% chance of a Fed rate hike this year, and Bank of America's peak-indicator model has 7 of 10 triggered. Watch funding and liquidity conditions as the accelerant, not the headline number.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $IREN, a two-sided setup, name both sides. The governance red flag first: the markets desk flagged the Roberts co-CEOs granted 18.2M RSUs combined, roughly $400M and 5.09% dilution, with the performance hurdles removed, against a share count that went from 100M to 357M and a $6B ATM (an at-the-market program that keeps selling new shares into the open market). That is the kind of thing that caps a multiple. The other side, from Chris: the 4-hour RSI is the most oversold in a year, and the prior two times it got here the stock rallied roughly 90% and then 130%, with price now backtesting broken downtrend resistance as support. A trade, not a marriage.
  • $PLTR, the anti-frontier-lab bet. Palantir was up around 7.5% expanding its Nvidia partnership for US-government AI systems. Alex Karp's pitch, quoted at length on the Financial Markets list, is that enterprises "pay for tokens that create no value" and are training their own replacements by running confidential data through frontier models, so the winners are the ones who let you own your compute, weights, and alpha. Whatever you think of Karp, that narrative is a real tailwind for the on-prem, data-sovereignty names.
  • $META, treat the "compute pivot" with suspicion. The bullish frame is Meta selling excess AI capacity like a neocloud. The skeptical read, which I lean toward, is that Google throttled Meta's Gemini compute, Meta is burning around $261M a month and just locked $40B across $NBIS, $CRWV, and Cursor, so "excess to sell" and "compute-constrained" cannot both be true. Reconcile the CoreWeave-scale contracts before you trust the story.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Yesterday's session recap (the semis flush, Meta's pop, the neocloud drop) was last night's story, so it is context here, not news. The All-In clip on SpaceX's valuation named no fund manager and no hard numbers, so there is nothing to trade in it. The quantum executive order is real but the loudest video on it named zero tickers, so it sits in News until someone attaches names. BowTied Bull's update is paywalled with no extractable specifics. Karp's full argument and the AI-access tightening are cross-routed to AI & Tools. The Senate-control shift and the Fed-personnel angle went to News.

item.title: [News] The midterm map moved toward Republicans, and the money noticed first
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Prediction markets now give the GOP a clear edge to hold the Senate, California cut its own AI deal ahead of Washington, and Russia is massing for a big strike on Ukraine.
item.content:encoded:
Prediction markets now give the GOP a clear edge to hold the Senate, California cut its own AI deal ahead of Washington, and Russia is massing for a big strike on Ukraine.

The theme this morning is power moving quietly, in the Senate map, at the Fed, and in who gets to gate frontier tech. The loud headlines are elsewhere. These are the ones that actually move.

1. The Senate picture swung, and the shift is the story. On Kalshi, Republicans are now 59% to hold the Senate, up 8 points since May 1, with Democrats down to 41% from 49%. That is the cleanest tradeable political move on the board and it happened without a single big headline, which is usually how the real ones move. Underneath the topline the races are more mixed than the number suggests: Jon Ossoff sits at 86% to hold Georgia, Maine has Platner tied with Collins, Ken Paxton leads in Texas at 58%, and in the Georgia governor's race Jackson leads at 54%. The wildest intraday move was the Colorado GOP gubernatorial primary, where Victor Marx ran from 57% to 90% in a session. Looking past the midterms, the 2028 Democratic nominee market is a genuine scrum: Newsom 22%, Ossoff at a new high of 14%, AOC 14%, Harris down to 8%. File the Senate shift as the one that matters now.

2. California didn't wait for Washington on AI. The return of Anthropic's flagship model from an 18-day export ban came with a governance tell worth flagging, per AI Central: California signed its own deal with Anthropic rather than wait for federal approval, and the redeployment arrived alongside a "consensus framework" that Anthropic is drafting with Amazon, Microsoft, and Google to score jailbreak severity, plus expanded US-government model testing. The through-line is that access to frontier AI is now a thing governments negotiate and gate, state by state and lab by lab, not a pure market. Palantir's Alex Karp spent the same news cycle needling the labs in public, arguing enterprises are paying for tokens that deliver no lasting value. The product mechanics of all this live in AI & Tools. The point for News is simpler: AI policy is now being written by states and standards bodies in real time.

3. Trump put quantum on the national-priority list. A new executive order pushes quantum computing to the center of US strategy, framed less as a funding announcement and more as a declaration that quantum is now a competitiveness and national-security priority on par with semiconductors, per Lark Davis. The pitch invokes problems that would take classical or AI systems ages, solved in minutes, across drug discovery, cryptography, and military logistics. Take the timelines with salt and note the order names no companies. The signal is the policy posture, not any specific stock, which is exactly why it sits here and not in Investing.

4. The Fed is changing character, quietly. The most underpriced institutional shift is at the Fed itself. The read on the Warsh regime is that it kills the print-to-print obsession, with Warsh reportedly pledging 2% inflation eight times in a single press conference and yields falling in response. That matters for how you read every data release for the next two years, trend over noise. The market is not fully buying the dovish story, Kalshi still prices a 50% chance of a rate hike this year. The tradeable market implications are in Investing.

5. Around the world, quickly. From Polymarket and the wires: Russia is readying a large drone and missile strike on Ukraine, reportedly up to 800 drones plus Iskander-M and Zircon missiles. On Taiwan, watch the capital, not the rhetoric. Wealthy Taiwanese are quietly parking assets in Singapore, and when the people with the most to lose move money first, that is the signal to price, not the press-conference noise. Crude flow through the Strait of Hormuz surged to roughly 10M barrels a day even as oil prices stayed soft. Markets abroad wobbled, with Korea's KOSPI down about 6% and the Korea Exchange halting program selling. On trade, the US declined to extend USMCA. And a 2025 disclosure showed Trump making up to $1.4B in stock purchases across roughly 22,000 trades, more than $635M of it tied to World Liberty Financial and his own meme coin. A sitting president, 22,000 trades, a personal coin. That one does not stay buried.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The memory-chip flush, the Bitcoin bleed, and Strategy trading below its Bitcoin stack are all market mechanics and live in Investing. The Fable 5 relaunch details, pricing, and prompting playbook went to AI & Tools. Nothing was dropped as noise from the news side this edition.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Fable 5 is back, and the free clock runs out July 7
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Anthropic redeployed its flagship with new guardrails that silently route sensitive work to Opus, and it published the exact habits to prompt it well.
item.content:encoded:
Anthropic redeployed its flagship with new guardrails that silently route sensitive work to Opus, and it published the exact habits to prompt it well.

Two things are true this morning: the best model is back, and access to it is getting rationed and gated. Everything below is about using the open window well and not getting caught depending on a switch someone else controls.

1. Fable 5 is live again, and the window to use it cheap is short. Per AI Central, Claude Fable 5 returned to global availability on July 1 after an 18-day export ban (out June 9, banned June 12, back July 1), redeployed with new classifiers that block cybersecurity tasks and a routing change where routine coding can silently fall back to Opus 4.8. Here is the part that is actually actionable, from Nate Herk: pricing is $10 per million input and $50 per million output, which is 2x Opus, the promotional period ends July 7, and on Claude plans you can only spend up to 50% of your weekly limits on Fable before it flips to usage credits. His honest take is that you realistically need Fable 5 for maybe 5 to 15% of your work, so do not burn the free window running it on everything. Use it on the hard things this week.

2. The prompting playbook went public, so take it. Herk pulled six habits straight from Anthropic's own docs, and they are the most useful thing in the feed today.

  • Give it the why, not bare commands.
  • Negative-prompt explicitly: "report what you find and stop, do not fix, send, or delete until I say go."
  • Let it act once it has enough: "when you have enough information to act, then act," rather than babysitting plan mode.
  • Make it prove it: "before you say something is done, point to the result that proves it, and if it is not verified, say so." Bake that into your skills and CLAUDE.md.
  • Do not ask Fable to show its reasoning. This one is Fable-specific. A standing "explain your reasoning" line can trip a safety refusal and quietly route the task to Opus 4.8.
  • Say less. Short instructions now steer as well as long rule dumps.

On effort, default to high, step up to x-high for hard problems, and know that Fable on low roughly matches Opus 4.8 on x-high for less money. Requests touching hacking, dangerous bio, or revealing private reasoning get shunted to Opus, and the API shows the switch even though the chat UI does not.

3. Five things to actually build with it. Chase AI leaned hard on his own course, so discount the urgency, but the use cases are concrete. Clone paid software (he rebuilt WhisperFlow into a local dictation app on Ollama). Audit your own Claude Code usage by pointing subagents at your session transcripts to cluster what should become a skill versus an automation versus nothing, run across 39 sessions. Run a full code review that turned 45 raw findings from four parallel reviewers into 24 deduped and ranked by severity. And the long-horizon proof point, open-source dev Brapholk built a browser Three.js game from a single hand-written PRD, with Fable 5 writing 21,000 lines of TypeScript across more than 90 commits. The pattern worth copying: plan with Opus and deep research, verify with Codex, then hand the long agentic build to Fable.

4. The counter-narrative: own your alpha, and enterprises are voting with their wallets. The sharpest argument this cycle came from Palantir's Alex Karp, quoted via Ricardo: enterprises "are paying for tokens that create no value," and if the labs truly delivered the value they claim, they would take equity or 30% of the profit instead of selling compute by the token. His deeper point is the scary one for anyone piping confidential data through a frontier model, you are training your own replacement while paying for the privilege. Whatever the spin, the behavior backs him up. On the Hermes list, Kimi K2.7 and GLM 5.2 both landed on GitHub Copilot and Cursor this week, with developers openly switching to cheaper Chinese models on cost. NVIDIA Research shipped Nemotron-Labs-TwoTower, a diffusion LM that keeps 98.7% of quality at 2.42x faster generation, and Not Diamond claims 30%-plus savings on coding agents through pure model routing with no quality loss. The theme across all of it: the frontier is getting gated and expensive, and the market is routing around it.

5. Own your memory and your ownership. Nate B Jones made the governance point that pairs with the Fable relaunch: the fastest way to make an AI agent dangerous is to let everyone use it and nobody own it. Any agent that can read your files, draft messages, change code, or update records needs an actual operational owner, not an org-chart one. As the tooling gets more capable and more autonomous this week, that is the unglamorous discipline that keeps it from quietly going wrong.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The MIT interview with Phillip Isola on why models converge was a genuinely good 72 minutes of representation-learning theory, but there is no this-week action in it, so I am holding it. The Sonnet 5 over-refusal complaints (treating email drafts as jailbreaks) are the same safety-overfit thread as the Fable-to-Opus fallback, worth watching as a pattern. The policy side of the Fable story, California signing its own deal with Anthropic and the Amazon/Microsoft/Google jailbreak-severity framework, went to News. Palantir as a tradeable name went to Investing.

item.title: [Investing] The memory trade cracked and Meta became a cloud company in one afternoon
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Chips got dumped while $META ripped 11% on a compute pivot, and Bitcoin printed a fresh low under $58K right on Ben Cowen's 2018 script.
item.content:encoded:
Chips got dumped while $META ripped 11% on a compute pivot, and Bitcoin printed a fresh low under $58K right on Ben Cowen's 2018 script.

Today was the rotation, live on the tape. If you have been waiting to see what "the leaders get caught offside" actually looks like, you just watched it happen in real time.

The close. Chips got taken to the woodshed while everything else held up. The semis led the drop, with the $SOX (the Philadelphia semiconductor index) off as much as 6.7% intraday:

  • Chip decliners: $MU down 6.7%, $ARM off 4.6%, $INTC down 4%, $AMD down 3.5%, $TSM off 3.4%, and even $NVDA down 2.3%.
  • The other side of the boat: mega-caps went green into the bell, $MSFT, $AAPL, $TSLA, $AMZN, and $GOOGL all near highs of day, and software ($IGV) up 4%.

Charlie Bilello's stat of the day: semis are up 237% in fourteen months, which now exceeds the dot-com-peak surge.

Under the tape it was a disinflation print. ISM Manufacturing landed at 53.3 (below the 54.0 expected but a sixth straight month of expansion), and prices-paid collapsed from 82.1 to 73.0, the biggest four-month drop in four years. ADP private payrolls came in soft at 98k against 118k expected. WTI crude slid under $68.50, a four-month low, with the entire Iran-war premium now gone.

Heads up on the calendar: US markets are closed Friday July 3 for Independence Day, so the real catalyst is Thursday's jobs report, pulled forward a day.

1. The memory air pocket showed up. For weeks the loudest trade in this book has been memory, and today it finally coughed. $MU gave back 6.7% on a day the whole complex bled. Do not confuse a shakeout with a top. The bull case is still intact and it is a good one: on Prof G Markets, Bernstein's Stacy Rasgon put roughly 70% of the semis rally down to actual earnings growth, not multiple expansion, and laid out why memory is the real bottleneck. HBM (high-bandwidth memory, the stacked chips that feed AI accelerators) eats four times the wafer capacity of standard DDR5, so adding wafers does not add bits, and clean-room shortage caps supply into 2027 and beyond. His read is the upcycle has duration and the "air pocket" is a 2028 problem, not a this-year problem. Milk Road Macro reminded everyone how far this has run: $MU +150%, Nebius $NBIS +124%, Hyperliquid $HYPE +114%, Bloom Energy $BE +110%, SK Square $SKSQ +63% over the stretch. When a trade is up like that and everyone can recite the thesis, days like today are the tax you pay. I am watching $MU into the high-70s gross-margin story, not selling the shakeout.

2. Meta became a cloud company, or the market decided it did. The single biggest move today was $META up 10 to 11% on "Meta Compute," reporting that it will sell its excess AI capacity AWS-Bedrock style and rent raw compute like a neocloud. Jim Cramer slapped a "$100 a share" on it. The bull frame is a capex-to-revenue bridge on a name trading around 9x earnings near a two-and-a-half-year low versus the $SPX. Here is the tell I would not ignore, flagged across the Financial Markets list by Jeff Pu and others: Meta just signed a $21B CoreWeave deal and Google is throttling Gemini compute, which makes Meta look compute-constrained, not oversupplied. So which is it, excess capacity to sell or a shortage to cover? You cannot be both. The neoclouds voted on the read: $CRWV fell 9.7 to 12% and $NBIS dropped 13% on the same headline. On the Market list, Amit Kukreja bought that $NBIS dip at $232.49 and called it an overreaction, noting AWS just raised GPU rents 20%. Reddit had the same instinct earlier, framing it as Zuck choosing violence against the neoclouds. My take: the pop is real, the "$100" is a meme, and the CoreWeave contract is the fact that has to be reconciled before I trust the excess-capacity story.

3. Bitcoin printed the low, right on schedule. $BTC made a fresh cycle low near $57,700 today, and two independent chartists I track called the same thing without talking to each other. Benjamin Cowen says 2026 is tracking 2018 almost tick for tick, the June 2018 low was $5,743 and today we tapped ~$5,770-scaled at $57.7K, hence his "simulation confirmed." His plan is unglamorous: ignore Bitcoin in the first half of a midterm year, DCA through the second half, and assume the bottom lands somewhere between $30K and $60K. Crypto Currently is looking at the same chart from the bull side: price is below the 200-week moving average (a once-every-four-years event), and for the first time since the 2022 bottom the weekly RSI printed a bullish divergence (lower low in price, higher low in momentum). Realized price (the average price at which every coin last moved, a proxy for the network's aggregate cost basis) sits at $53,500, and Tether ($USDT) just flipped Ethereum ($ETH) by market cap for the first time ever, which is the kind of thing that happens at washouts. The flows back the fear: Degen Sensei notes US spot BTC ETFs just booked a record $4.5B of June outflows, the worst month on record. Kalshi has only 12% odds Bitcoin hits $100K this year (an all-time low) and 70% odds it trades below $50K. Both creators are DCA-buying under $60K and neither is going all in. I am doing the same. This looks like an oversold bounce setup, not a confirmed trend reversal. Watch for a weekly higher low to confirm a July relief rally into the 200-day, and watch ETF outflows flipping positive as the real bottom signal.

4. Solana is the relative-strength name in crypto. If you want the coin that is acting best, it is $SOL, holding around $77 to $79 while Bitcoin makes new lows. Lark Davis gives the actionable level: a daily close at or above $76 opens a run at $97, roughly 28%. Two well-known traders (Psycho, who publicly bought the $15 Solana bottom last cycle, and OSF) are both accumulating spot here and calling it a "lock in now" zone. Just respect that if Bitcoin pukes again, Solana rolls over with it, so you are buying leveraged beta on a Bitcoin bounce here.

5. Tomorrow is a Tesla day. $TSLA reports Q2 deliveries pre-market Thursday July 2, and the gap between estimates is enormous: the Street is around 406k, Troy Teslike is at 466k. The stock is up 16% since Friday and pinned near $400, which happens to be the level where Michael Burry's short sits on the wrong side. Reclaim it cleanly and the momentum crowd targets $440 then $520. This is a binary, so size it like one. The read-through worth noting: $GM's US EV sales fell 41% year over year in Q2, so the EV pie is not the story, Tesla's own number is.

Single names worth your watchlist. A few high-conviction ideas landed today that fit a position-builder better than the day's noise:

  • The BEDS trade ($GEO + $CXW). The detention-bed thesis is the cleanest catalyst setup I saw. Federal agencies need roughly ten turnkey facilities, GEO Group and CoreCivic each sit on 20,000-plus idle beds, and both trade at $3 to $4B caps against a $70B funding backdrop. $CXW around $30.50 and $GEO around $29.50, both already up 30 to 45% since June. Benchmark puts 80% odds on a two-facility sale with price targets to $36, Northland sees $40 on both. Both just beat on EPS and raised guidance. Call-to-put volume is running 30-to-1. Risk is a policy-shutdown headline, which knocked them 20% earlier this year.
  • BlackBerry ($BB). The least-covered name with the strongest fundamental turn. QNX is the safety-certified OS in 275 million-plus vehicles and the foundation under NVIDIA's DRIVE and Thor platforms. Revenue up 26%, adjusted EBITDA up 144%, and the first positive operating cash flow in a first quarter in nine years. A real physical-AI edge-OS play, not a meme.
  • Figure ($FIGR) and Ouster ($OUST). Two fresh IPOs riding structural themes. $FIGR is Mike Cagney's blockchain-native lending marketplace, 55% EBITDA margins, HELOC origination costs of ~$800 versus an ~$10K industry average, ran from $40 to $78 on preliminary results. $OUST is a digital-lidar and perception platform for physical AI, Q1 revenue ~$49M up 49%, $175M cash and no debt, around $42 with a 12-month base case of $60 to $75. Both are swing-to-position candidates, not day trades.
  • The beaten-down mega-cap dip-buys. Invest with Henry is buying $SOFI under $18 (net income +134% year over year), $PLTR around $100 (half off its peak), $NVDA around $200 (target $300), and $MSFT at $373 (down 17% in a month). Fade the affiliate pitch, keep the names on the list.
  • Real-cash-flow crypto. With momentum bleeding, Milk Road is paying up for revenue: Galaxy Digital ($GLXY), whose "Helios" site is a 1.6 GW campus leased to CoreWeave for fifteen years at $1B-plus a year, and Sky ($SKY), the USDS stablecoin issuer that just did its best quarter ever at ~$124M revenue while the token fell 25%. The Milk Road Pro desk is adding $GLXY on the dip and rotating Micron profits into Marvell ($MRVL) as the next bottleneck link.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The threadguy and "Fable tokens" livestreams had no transcript, so nothing to mine there. Trader Mayne's zero-tax-country monologue and the a16z summer reading list were lifestyle and books, dropped. The energy-and-photonics bottleneck ($BE, $LITE, $GLW, $NBIS, $MRVL, and the 80-to-150 GW data-center power ramp) is a genuine investing theme but I routed the detail to AI & Tools this edition since it rode in on the AI-compute story. The rare-earth choke-point angle went to News.

item.title: [News] Trump wants cheaper gas he can't deliver, and Iran is the reason
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Crude cracked a four-month low as the war premium bled out, David Sacks says the DSA now runs the Democratic base, and the prediction markets moved.
item.content:encoded:
Crude cracked a four-month low as the war premium bled out, David Sacks says the DSA now runs the Democratic base, and the prediction markets moved.

Oil is the thread running through the whole day. Crude cracked a four-month low as the Iran war premium bled out, Trump tried to jawbone gas prices he cannot actually move, and the prediction markets swung toward Republicans holding the Senate. Here is what actually moved.

1. The gas-price theater. President Trump went on Truth Social today demanding that gas retailers cut prices because they are "too high," which Prof G Markets correctly filed under break-something-then-blame-someone-else economics. Here is the actual chain of events. Gas is elevated because of the Iran conflict, and the "memorandum of understanding" the administration claimed had settled it is not holding. The US and Iran traded strikes over the weekend, each side accusing the other of breaking the deal, and the Strait of Hormuz risk is unresolved. The irony is that the market already did the work Trump is demanding: WTI crude slid under $68.50 today, a four-month low, and traders say the entire war premium has now bled out, with crude flirting with contango (near-term barrels priced below later-dated ones, a sign of ample supply) per Javier Blas. Prices will not fall on a social-media post. They will fall when the conflict actually ends.

2. David Sacks says the socialists already won the Democratic base. On All-In, David Sacks argued that the Democratic Socialists of America have effectively taken over the Democratic Party, controlling the base and "where all the energy is." He read out what he says is the DSA platform, abolish the Senate, subordinate the courts to Congress, public ownership of major corporations, and defund the "Department of War," and quoted a DSA co-chair saying the quiet part out loud: "We're using the Democratic Party as a ballot-access vehicle, not because we share its goals. We see the democratic establishment as an obstacle, not a home." Take Sacks' framing with the salt it deserves, he is an interested party. But "public ownership of major corporations" gaining a foothold in a major party's platform is exactly the kind of tail risk investors ignore until it is priced, so file it.

3. The prediction markets moved. From the Kalshi and Polymarket lists: Republicans are now 59% to hold the Senate, the highest since March 1. In the New Jersey seventh, Democrat Rebecca Bennett sits at 79% versus Kean at 21%. The Colorado GOP governor primary is a coin flip, Victor Marx around 57% against Kirkmeyer. On monetary policy, only about 20% odds that Kevin Warsh's Fed hikes at the next meeting, though Bank of America's Brian Moynihan is the loud dissent calling for three hikes. Trump is on CNBC tomorrow at 5PM and said he wants to "declassify almost everything." Watch the Warsh-hike odds, because a hawkish surprise is the one thing that would spoil the disinflation story the market is currently leaning on.

4. The rare-earth choke point is the story under all the AI stories. Capital Flows, with guest Adam Rossi, made the case that the physical world is back at the center of markets. COVID and now AI pulled capital into hardware, materials, and physical supply chains, and the US position in critical minerals and rare-earth magnets is fragile. Rare earths sit under both defense systems and AI hardware, which means a single policy headline can ripple straight through the metal stocks and the whole space-and-defense industrial buildout that rests on top of energy and metals. One export restriction out of Beijing reprices the entire chain.

5. Around the world, quickly. Sweden ordered Google to pay Klarna $1.97B. Binance is running monthly net outflows above $1.8B and is suspending services in the EU as the MiCA deadline hits, pushing crypto founders toward Dubai. NATO is pushing a 5%-of-GDP defense-spending target and the UK added roughly $20B in defense. Japan is planning a domestic AI model and a stated goal of 10 million AI robots by 2040, and Sony said PlayStation goes digital-only from 2028. Small items on their own, but they rhyme: rearmament, sovereignty over compute, and a hard pivot to the physical and strategic.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The market mechanics of today's session (the semis-to-mega-cap rotation, $META's compute pivot, Bitcoin's new low) all live in Investing. The AI-governance and open-source-model developments went to AI & Tools. Nothing was dropped as noise from the news side this edition.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Fable 5 is back, and the smart move is to own your memory, not rent it
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Anthropic redeployed its flagship globally amid a caps-and-refusals backlash, the Skills-over-files shift went mainstream, and energy quietly became the AI bottleneck that matters.
item.content:encoded:
Anthropic redeployed its flagship globally amid a caps-and-refusals backlash, the Skills-over-files shift went mainstream, and energy quietly became the AI bottleneck that matters.

Fable 5 is back online, but the bigger story is architectural: the sharpest operators are all moving to own their memory and skills layer and just rent the model on top. Add two quiet Codex upgrades and a hard reality check on agent governance. Here is the rundown.

1. Fable 5 is live again, with guardrails and grumbling. Anthropic is redeploying Claude Fable 5 globally, this time wired with new cybersecurity classifiers, and routine coding requests fall back to Opus 4.8. The community reaction has been loud, with well-known accounts complaining about usage caps and over-eager refusals, and Anthropic reportedly drafting a jailbreak-severity framework alongside Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. Alex Finn posted an early playbook with a couple of real ideas buried in the hype (he leans hard on a paid deposit tool, so discount accordingly): use Fable to audit every pull request merged while it was gone, drive the Claude-for-Chrome extension to walk your app like a real user and write a UX report, and use the `/loop` command (for example `/loop 24h`) to repeatedly hammer your own API endpoints for vulnerabilities, since API routes are the biggest attack surface on vibe-coded apps. That last one is the genuinely useful idea in the video.

2. The real shift: own your memory, rent the intelligence. Two of the sharpest operators in this space are saying the same thing this week. Ruben Hassid, who has built a following on Claude Cowork guides, is now telling people to ditch files and folders entirely (they "leak," Claude reads output folders even when told not to and poisons its own context) in favor of Skills plus Projects only. A Skill is a capability you invoke with a slash command like `/linkedin-post` or `/contract-drafting`, and it is portable across Gemini and ChatGPT. His settings advice:

  • Turn almost everything off, keep only Connectors on.
  • Run Opus 4.8 on High, and step up to Max effort or Fable 5 when the task earns it.
  • Start a fresh session every twenty or so messages, because message thirty can cost thirty-one times message one in tokens. Nate B Jones makes the structural version of the argument: rent the model, own the memory. His stack is OpenBrain for memory, OpenSkills for portable methods, and OpenEngine to orchestrate across Claude, Codex, ChatGPT, Kimi, and Gemini, and his point is that the barrier to building this yourself has dropped roughly fivefold since February. If a flagship gets locked behind regulatory approval, the person who owns their memory and skills layer barely notices.

3. Codex quietly shipped two great features. From The AI Advantage: OpenAI's Codex now has record-and-replay. Hit record, perform any computer workflow for up to thirty minutes while it captures your screen and microphone, and Codex turns it into a reusable Skill you can replay or schedule. Nate B Jones also flags that Codex now has an auto-review pass, so a "draft this" instruction will not accidentally send, a direct answer to the cautionary tale of an agent that mailed an insurance appeal it was only supposed to write. The theme across both: agents are getting the boring safety and repeatability features that make them usable for real work.

4. A concrete workflow worth stealing: contract review. Dylan Davis laid out a clean four-prompt method that works in Claude or ChatGPT. Decode the document to plain fifth-grade English from your side's perspective, expose the unusual terms and the missing standard protections with exact quotes ranked by impact, force a citation check with the instruction "a made-up answer is three times worse to me than a blank one, label anything not in the document as not found," then draft the friendly redline email. For anything over fifty pages, start a fresh chat and treat every finding as unconfirmed. The citation-check prompt is the step most people skip and the one that keeps the model honest.

5. Agents as labor, and the standard to find them. Greg Isenberg argues the next wave of software sells work, not tools, a multi-trillion-dollar labor market rather than a seat-license market, and his examples (Slang AI as a restaurant host, Same Day for home-services dispatch) point at outcome-based pricing like $2K setup plus $30 per qualified appointment. The plumbing for that world took a step forward with the Agentic Resource Discovery spec (ARD v0.9), covered by Jordamøn, published by eleven companies including Microsoft, Google, GitHub, Hugging Face, and NVIDIA under the same Linux Foundation body where Anthropic donated MCP (the Model Context Protocol, the now-standard way agents plug into tools and data). It is a yellow pages for agents: publish a static `ai-catalog.json` at a well-known path and registries crawl it like search engines. The catch is telling, a census the day after launch found zero of the thirty-nine surveyed domains actually serving the file. Strong backing, no adoption yet. Watch whether publishers actually host it.

6. The counterweight: governance and sustainability. For all the "ninety percent of code is AI-generated" hype, Mark Ajzenstadt supplies the reality check with real telemetry. 72% of enterprises claim production agentic AI, but 60% have no formal governance and only one in five has a mature model. Across 22,000 developers, AI lifts throughput but raises change-failure rates: 30% more code, median PR review time up 441%, and 31% more pull requests merged with zero human review. His fix is a four-layer stack (gateway, control, tracing, impact) and his warning is that a board deck can look transformed while the org is identical. Pair that with the Reddit skeptics arguing AI is still in a pilot phase where lab revenue trails compute cost, and you have the honest bear case on how fast this actually compounds. On the industry clock, Prof G thinks an OpenAI IPO is more likely late 2026 or the first half of 2027 than 2027-and-beyond.

7. Cross-route to Investing: energy is the bottleneck now. The most investable AI signal this edition is about power, not models. Per the Limitless Podcast, US data-center demand is projected to go from roughly 80 GW in 2026 to 150 GW in 2028, power is only about 10% of a data-center budget but 100% of the bottleneck, and grid power carries a five-year lead time. New Vera Rubin racks pull over 100 kW, ten times the prior generation. The named plays: Bloom Energy ($BE) for on-site fuel cells in 90 days, Lumentum ($LITE) and Corning ($GLW) for the optical interconnects that replace melting copper (NVIDIA put roughly $2B into $LITE and $3.2B into $GLW), Marvell ($MRVL) for power delivery and custom silicon, and Nebius ($NBIS) as the neocloud with a ~$50B contract backlog. A Reddit thesis makes the same photonics-and-light argument from the retail side. The full ticker detail sits in today's Investing edition.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Every-Codex video had no transcript available, so it is held for a re-fetch. The m0h open-source repo list (anthropics/skills, openai/openai-agents-python, google/adk-python, xai-org/grok-1, deepseek-ai/deepseek-v3, qwenlm/qwen3, and roughly twenty more) is a solid bookmark if you build, but it is a reference dump rather than news. The energy and photonics names above are cross-routed to Investing for the tradeable detail.

item.title: [Investing] The memory trade ate the first half, and now Burry is shorting the melt-up into July
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Stocks closed the best six months in years on a record-narrow semis bid, Michael Burry stacked shorts on Nvidia Tesla and Caterpillar, and Bitcoin lost its 200-week line as the forced-seller math around Saylor got real
item.content:encoded:
Stocks closed the best six months in years on a record-narrow semis bid, Michael Burry stacked shorts on Nvidia Tesla and Caterpillar, and Bitcoin lost its 200-week line as the forced-seller math around Saylor got real

Half the year is in the books and it was a monster. The Dow finished H1 up 8 percent, the S&P 500 up 8 percent, the Nasdaq up 11 percent, and the Russell 2000 up 21 percent for its best first half since 1991 (Financial Markets list). Q2 alone added roughly $8 trillion of market cap to the S&P, the best quarter since 2020 (Market list). Great number. Now read who carried it, because that is the whole story heading into July.

The only trade that mattered was memory, and that is both the bull case and the risk

One group did the work. H1's S&P leaderboard: $SNDK up 857 percent, $MU up 303 percent, $INTC up 278 percent, $WDC up 271 percent, $MRVL up 250 percent, $DELL up 243 percent, $AMD up 171 percent (Financial Markets list). The semis index $SOX ran 83 percent in Q2 against 9 percent for the Mag 7, a full rotation out of the mega-cap generals and into the picks-and-shovels (People to follow list). Three of the new members of the $1 trillion club this quarter were memory names. Semiconductors are now a record 19.7 percent of the entire S&P 500 (Polymarket list).

That is roughly a fifth of the index riding on one theme. Jim Cramer is out saying $MU "tells its story better" than $NVDA, and Traderstewie has $MU as his IBD Top 50 number one with a $1,400 to $1,500 target (Market list). When the leadership is this narrow and this loved, you own it and you respect it at the same time. SK Hynix just filed to list in the US under $SKHY, one more sign of how crowded this memory trade has gotten.

Michael Burry just shorted the melt-up

Michael Burry is now short $NVDA, $TSLA, and $CAT, the first time he has been on Caterpillar, confirmed across multiple lists (Market list). Burry is early and often wrong on timing, so this is not a sell signal by itself. But shorting Nvidia, Tesla, and the single most cyclical industrial name in the market at once is a coherent bet that the melt-up is late-cycle. Pair it with the accounting story running underneath the tape. A University of Florida study says roughly 12 percent of Q1 S&P earnings were unrealized markups on OpenAI and Anthropic stakes rather than real operating profit (Prof G Markets). Strip the paper gains and earnings growth falls back to the five-year average. The melt-up is real. The quality underneath it is thinner than the tape suggests.

Bitcoin broke, and the Saylor math is the story now

Crypto is the opposite picture and it is ugly. Bitcoin closed below its 200-week moving average (the four-year trend line that has floored past bear markets) for the first time since 2023, down about 20 percent in June and 53 percent from its all-time high, trading in the high $50Ks to low $60Ks (Kalshi list). Whales are pressing it. On Hyperliquid there are three separate leveraged shorts worth more than $100 million combined, including a 20x position of 1,100 BTC with a liquidation up at $67,914 (Hermes list).

The bottom-callers cluster lower. Trader Mayne wants $49,500 to $50,000 first and would not be shocked by a flush to $38,000 to $40,000 into Q4, and he is only a buyer heavy in the high $30Ks to high $40Ks (Trader Mayne). His cycle model caps the next bull run near $180,000, not the $500,000 everyone spent last year screaming about. Kaleo says low 50s into the 40s. Polymarket treats sub-$50K as almost a certainty.

The variable that could actually mark the low is Michael Saylor. Strategy ($MSTR) is now something like $14 to $16 billion underwater on its stack with an average cost far above spot, and it owes roughly $1.7 billion a year in perpetual preferred dividends it can pause but not stop (threadguy). Kaleo is arguing Saylor should proactively sell $1.25 billion of BTC now (Hermes list). If a new monetization program forces Saylor to actually sell Bitcoin to fund those dividends, that is the FTX-style forced-seller capitulation that tends to print the bottom. Watch it.

The bull who is buying the knife

The other side has a name and a balance sheet. Ben Werkman, CIO of Strive ($ASST, the Vivek Ramaswamy-founded treasury firm run by ex-CalPERS bond chief Matt Cole), is buying, close to 20,000 BTC on the books after picking up 2,500 then 1,500 coins in the $60Ks (Milk Road). His pitch is the 200-week and power-law floor as an entry, fiat debasement at 6.7 percent M2 growth, and a sovereign bearer-asset thesis. Strive's preferred $SATA pays a 13 percent dividend daily and trades around $90 against $100 par, and Werkman blames the discount on a leverage unwind in Strategy's STRC paper, not credit. He held an 18-month dividend reserve and sat out last week's mess. If you want the bull case articulated by someone with skin in it, that is it.

Names on the tape worth a look

  • $UWMC (UWM Holdings) is down about 80 percent from its 2024 high to around $2, at capitulation volume, and tomorrow July 2 there is a Two Harbors merger vote that would improve its leverage (r/UWMC). It holds 44 percent wholesale market share and originates 10 to 12 percent of all US mortgages, trading at a base-case P/E under 5. The risk is CEO Mat Ishbia, who pledged more than half his stake (805 million shares) to JPMorgan and has pulled $4.4 billion out of the company since 2021. He also stopped selling, last hitting the tape at $3.39 in May.
  • $NOK (Nokia) is a stealth AI-networking play. Nvidia put $1 billion into it, it bought Infinera for optical, and connecting tens of thousands of GPUs is exactly its lane (r/Nokia).
  • $RKLB (Rocket Lab) is acquiring Iridium ($IRDM) in a roughly $8 billion deal (Market list). $AMBA popped 22 percent on edge-AI silicon. $NBIS ran 175 percent in Q2. $EOSE (Eos Energy, zinc-based long-duration storage) is moving from proving the tech to proving management can execute (r/Eos). $IONQ stays the leading pure-play quantum bet with the valuation priced for perfection (Rick Orford).
  • $SOL has two independent bulls. Market Bubble sees a run from $60 to roughly $600 this cycle on a break of the prior all-time high (Market Bubble), and Trader Mayne is long from about $67. ETH at roughly $1,570 gets a $6,000 fair-value case from DeFi Dad, who values it as the cost to attack the network securing $160 billion in stablecoins and $200 billion in DeFi (DeFi Dad).

Levels and the trader tape

Stocks with Josh has SPY confirmed bullish above 743 and pushing 757, with a summer flash-crash near 19 percent the dip he actually wants to buy (Stocks with Josh). Tesla lives and dies at $411, reclaim it and 425.50 then 450 open up, lose it and $370 is in play (Stocks with Josh). His Microsoft call already hit the 3624 target. And over in the casino, an r/wallstreetbets trader is calling $NVDA "free money" into incoming VIX volatility to kick off a $1,000 account challenge (Nightcrawler). File that under sentiment, not analysis.

Market context: what to watch into the open

Q3 opens on the back of the best first half since the early 90s, and July is historically kind. Ryan Detrick notes the S&P has been higher in July 11 straight years for a 2.9 percent average, and 5 for 5 under Trump (Financial Markets list). The Dow closed above 52,000 for the first time. The catalyst that matters this week is Thursday's jobs report, and after JOLTS openings ran hot at 7.594 million versus 7.296 million expected and Fed's Hammack called the labor market "right around full employment," a softer payrolls print is the good outcome that eases the hawkish worry (People to follow list). Also on the radar: the Two Harbors and $UWMC vote tomorrow, whether the SK Hynix $SKHY IPO adds fuel to the memory bid, and the reaction function under new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh. Key level to hold is SPY 743. The tape is strong, the leadership is narrow, and the smartest bear in the room just shorted three of the biggest names at once. I stay long the memory trade and keep the stop tight.

Filtered / Cross-Routed

  • OpenAI and Anthropic IPO mechanics, the AI-bubble earnings study, and the Anthropic export-control news are led in AI & Tools, with the market read kept here.
  • Trump's $1.4 billion crypto disclosure and the Colorado primary results route to News, with the $DJT and stablecoin angles noted here.
  • Dropped: a promo-heavy live MSFT/TSLA options call and the "how Camilla Araujo invests $30 million" clip (real estate, not a market call).

item.title: [News] Colorado just fired a 15-term Democrat, and the primary purge is only getting started
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Diana DeGette became the fifth House Democrat to lose a primary as Colorado handed insurgents a clean sweep, the Court reshaped who a president can fire, and Trump disclosed more than $1.4 billion in crypto income
item.content:encoded:
Diana DeGette became the fifth House Democrat to lose a primary as Colorado handed insurgents a clean sweep, the Court reshaped who a president can fire, and Trump disclosed more than $1.4 billion in crypto income

The story this morning is a primary bloodbath, and it is not close. Colorado handed the establishment a clean loss up and down the ballot, the Supreme Court quietly rewrote the rules on presidential firing power, and Trump's financial disclosure put a jaw-dropping number on his crypto wealth. Here is what actually happened.

Colorado torched its incumbents

Diana DeGette, a 15-term Democrat, was unseated by Melat Kiros, who closed at 99 percent on Kalshi (Kalshi list). That makes her the fifth House Democrat to lose a primary this cycle, and the market says there is a 69 percent chance at least seven House Democrats go down before it is over. Five incumbents deep, this is a purge with momentum.

The rest of Colorado went insurgent too. On the Republican side, Kirkmeyer sits at 95 percent for governor after Victor Marx collapsed from 90 percent to 7 percent. On the Democratic side, John Hickenlooper is at 99 percent for Senate and Phil Weiser at 99 percent for governor. When the favorites move this hard this fast, somebody knew something the polls did not.

The 2026 governor map is a dead heat

Zoom out to the 36 governor races and the whole board splits 25 to 25 (Election Time). Here is the board:

  • Red pickups: Vivek Ramaswamy leads Ohio by 1.3 over Amy Acton, Byron Donalds leads Florida by 3.4 over David Jolly, and Kansas flips red with Ty Masterson favored at 71 percent.
  • Blue walls cruising: Josh Shapiro up 22.5 in Pennsylvania, JB Pritzker up 20 in Illinois and already eyeing 2028, and Gavin Newsom's California seat going to Xavier Becerra up 24.
  • Genuine toss-ups: Arizona (Katie Hobbs plus 4.3 over Andy Biggs), Wisconsin (Mandela Barnes plus 4 over Tom Tiffany), and Michigan (Jocelyn Benson plus 3.6 over John James). One honest caveat from the source: the polls have been overestimating Democrats in Iowa and Georgia, so read those leans with a discount.

The Court reshaped presidential firing power

The Supreme Court overruled Trump's attempt to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook, protecting the central bank, but in the same breath expanded the president's firing power at other agencies (Prof G Markets). So the Fed stays walled off while the rest of the executive branch gets more exposed to the Oval Office. The Court is also pegged at 95 percent to strike down Trump's birthright-citizenship ban (Polymarket list). The bench is walling off the Fed and the Constitution while handing Trump a longer leash over the rest of the executive branch.

Markets crossover: Trump's crypto disclosure is staggering

Trump's 2025 financial disclosure ran more than 800 pages and reported over $1.4 billion in crypto income, $515 million from World Liberty Financial token sales and $635 million in meme-coin royalties, with his single largest holding being 114.75 million shares of $DJT (Financial Markets list). He also disclosed $86.5 million in media legal settlements (Polymarket list). A sitting president earning nine figures in meme-coin royalties is a genuinely new thing in American politics. The market angle on $DJT and stablecoins sits in Investing.

Around the world

  • Iran is selling oil at 20 percent above its prior price and asserting sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz, which helped push crude higher (Market list).
  • Netanyahu vowed no withdrawal from Lebanon (Polymarket list).
  • The Venezuela earthquake death toll climbed to 1,943.
  • The World Bank will phase out lending to China by 2031, a slow but real decoupling signal.

Health and culture: the height obsession goes mainstream

The cultural thread of the morning is heightmaxxing. Roughly 1 million South Korean teenagers are now on human growth hormone for height, with about 60 percent of pediatric HGH going toward growth rather than medical treatment in a market worth around $350 million (threadguy). The driver is the brutal correlation that 58 percent of Fortune 500 CEOs are six feet or taller versus 15 percent of American men. The counterintuitive science, for anyone doing the math on their kids: making a kid taller with HGH does not meaningfully shorten their lifespan, and height on net is slightly protective, improving lung function and lipid profiles even as it carries a mild cancer-risk premium from having more cells (Cremieux).

Elsewhere in culture: the FDA is now allowing Zyn to market itself as "less harmful" (Polymarket list), AI music got a little dystopian as one creator generated a passable "threadguy Drake" song in 20 seconds and then found 238 real songs sitting in the training set, and LeBron James is now 75 percent to join the Warriors and will not play for the Lakers (Kalshi list).

Filtered / Cross-Routed

  • The investable pieces (memory/semis, Bitcoin, single names) live in Investing, and the AI-model and IPO news in AI & Tools.
  • Trump's crypto disclosure is reported here with the trading angle noted in Investing.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Commerce lifts the leash on Claude, OpenAI freezes its IPO, and the AI bubble shows up in the earnings
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Department of Commerce restored export access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5, OpenAI pushed its IPO toward 2027 while Anthropic races to list first, and a new study says a chunk of S&P profit is just AI paper gains
item.content:encoded:
The Department of Commerce restored export access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5, OpenAI pushed its IPO toward 2027 while Anthropic races to list first, and a new study says a chunk of S&P profit is just AI paper gains

Three stories run the AI tape this morning and they are all about access and money, not benchmarks. Commerce took the leash off Anthropic's frontier models. OpenAI slammed the brakes on its IPO. And a finance professor put a number on how much of the market's AI profit is imaginary. Benchmarks can wait.

Commerce restored export access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5

The biggest catalyst of the session: the Department of Commerce lifted the export controls on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, and access is restoring (Market list). Politico had flagged the easing was coming. This showed up across five separate feeds this morning, so it is real and it matters. The models that were throttled are coming back online, which unwinds one of the biggest overhangs on Anthropic right as the company is trying to walk into public markets. If you use the Claude frontier tier, expect capacity to loosen.

OpenAI hit pause on its IPO, and Anthropic is racing to go first

OpenAI filed a confidential S-1 on June 8 and was on track for a fall debut. Now it is pushing toward 2027 (Prof G Markets). The reasons are unflattering. Sam Altman wants a $1 trillion valuation, CFO Sarah Friar said the company is not ready, and Alex Heath's reporting describes the finance and investor-relations org as not in place, disclosures as "a mess," Sam owning no equity, and governance "all over the place." SpaceX's rocky IPO day spooked them into waiting.

That opens the door for Anthropic to list first, with people eyeing October or November and Kalshi putting the odds of Anthropic going public this year at 76 percent (Prof G Markets). The logic is simple and a little brutal. The third AI IPO gets less demand than the second, so whoever goes first wins the scarcity premium. Scott Galloway reframes the real question as whether these deals even "get away," since both firms can raise enough privately to never need the public market (Prof G Markets). If they keep the ship in harbor, that tells you the window closed.

The AI bubble is now visible in the accounting

University of Florida finance professor Baolin Wang found that Q1 2026 S&P earnings were inflated by roughly 12 percent (Prof G Markets). Alphabet, Amazon, and Nvidia booked a combined $69 billion in profit from unrealized gains on their OpenAI and Anthropic stakes, run through "other income" under accounting standard ASU 2016-01, not from selling anything. Strip out the markups and S&P earnings growth drops to about 16 percent, right in line with the five-year average. Private AI valuations now account for roughly 12 cents of every profit dollar in the S&P. Wang's line: "our main bubble indicator is now also in a bubble." Strip the paper gains and the market's earnings story is running at trend, not breaking out.

Models: the frontier keeps moving

  • GPT-5.6 is projected to ship within 10 days at 61 percent odds on Polymarket (Polymarket list). Anthropic already shipped Claude Sonnet 5 and a Claude Science app.
  • A real capability milestone: GPT-5.5 Pro paired with Claude Opus 4.8 resolved 9 open math problems, including 4 from COLT, 1 from FOCS, and 4 in commutative algebra (Hermes list). Frontier models solving genuinely open problems is the story under the IPO noise.

Tooling: Midjourney walks into healthcare

Midjourney, a 40-person bootstrapped shop doing $200 million in revenue and highly profitable, is plowing cash into preventative medicine (AI News and Strategy Daily). The product is a cheap ultrasound device that images the body far faster than an MRI, which they are calling the biggest medical-imaging breakthrough in 50 years, with a goal of roughly 1 billion scans a year within a couple of years. Big claim from an image-generation company. What separates it from vaporware is that Midjourney throws off enough cash to actually fund it.

Compute: the picks-and-shovels bid, plus a Leopold sighting

The money is chasing whoever can serve cheap inference and power it. Leopold Aschenbrenner's Situational Awareness took a 19.9 percent stake in Sharon AI ($SHAZ), an Australian neocloud with a 72MW Nvidia deal and roughly $1.3 billion in ARR (annual recurring revenue), deliberately staying just under the 20 percent equity-method accounting line (Crypto list). Bloom Energy's AI-power deal got expanded from $5 billion to $25 billion (Financial Markets list). Nvidia put $1 billion into Nokia for AI networking. And the counterweight to all the hiring hype: Microsoft is laying off more than 5,000 (Polymarket list). The AI capex boom and the AI headcount cut are happening in the same company at the same time. The spend goes to GPUs, not headcount.

Filtered / Cross-Routed

  • The investable read on the memory/semis trade, IonQ, Nokia, and Bloom lives in Investing.
  • The Anthropic export-control news is a genuine dual-relevant item and is led here with the market angle noted in Investing.
